coredata.dc:title: “On-off-on” detection of Fe3+ and F−, biological imaging, and its logic gate operation based on excitation-independent blue-fluorescent carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Lin
Shi, Lihong
Jia, Jing
Jiao, Yuan
Gao, Yifang
Liu, Yang
Dong, Chuan
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescent nanoprobe based on carbon dots (CDs) has been facilely synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal pyrolysis of salicylic acid and utilized for the sequential detection of Fe3+ and F− in vitro. The fluorescence of CDs can be extinguished dramatically by Fe3+ based on static quenching and subsequently recovery upon addition of F− due to the formation of stabler FeF3. The probe exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity toward Fe3+ and F− with a good linearity in the range of 10–300 μM and 0.1–200 μM, respectively, and a low detection limit of 52 nM and 8.5 nM, respectively. More importantly, as-prepared CDs with exceedingly fluorescence stability, negligible toxicity and superior biocompatibility have been expanded for detection Fe3+ and F− in living cell and Escherichia coli. Furthermore, an “AND” logic gate based on as-obtained CDs has been constructed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fe3+
F−
Probe
Imaging
Logic gate

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fe3+detection of CDs in aqueous solution
F− detection of CDs in aqueous solution
Detection of Fe3+ and F− in real samples
Fe3+ detection of CDs in living cells and E. coli cells
Logic operation of CDs towards Fe3+ and F−

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CDs (A) scale bar is 50 nm, (B) scale bar is 20 nm, (C) scale bar is 10 nm, and (D) the size distribution of CDs.
(A) Raman spectrum of CDs. (B) XPS spectrum of CDs. (C) C1s and (D) O1s spectra of CDs. (E) FT-IR spectrum of CDs.
(A) UV–vis absorption (black line), PL excitation (blue line), and emission (red line) spectra of CDs. (B) Excitation-independent PL behavior of CDs.

(A) Selectivity of the CDs for Fe3+ over other ionic species under pH 7.0 conditions at 320 nm excitation ([Mn+] = 300 μM). The blue bars represent the addition of metal ions (300 μM for all the metal ions) to CDs solution. The black bars represent the subsequent addition of 300 μM Fe3+ to CDs solution. (B) Representative fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs in the presence of various concentrations of Fe3+ (10 μM–300 μM). Insert: The relationship between ΔF and Fe3+ concentrations from 10 μM to 300 μM (ΔF = F0 − F, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively). The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements.

(A) Fluorescence response of coexisting substances (0.1 mM) and the mixture of the coexisting substances with F− under pH 7.0 conditions at 320 nm excitation. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@Fe3+ upon addition of various concentrations of F− (from top to bottom, 0–400 μM). Insert: The relationship between ΔF and F− concentrations from 0.1 μM to 200 μM (ΔF = F − F0, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the CDs@Fe3+ in the absence and presence of F−, respectively). The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements.
Dynamic fluorescence imaging of SMMC-7721 cells with CDs@Fe3+ and CDs@Fe3+@ F−, the cell images were recorded at λex/λem of 405/450 ± 25 nm.

Laser scanning confocal microscopy images of SMMC-7721 cells incubated with 0.58 mg mL−1 CDs at 37 °C for 0.5 h. The first, second and third panels are cell images taken at λex/λem of 405/450 ± 25, 488/525 ± 25 and 543/650 ± 25 nm, respectively. The fourth, fifth and sixth panels are the merged images of the first and second panels, second and third panels, and first and third panels, respectively. (A), (B) and (C) are LSCM fluorescence imaging of SMMC7721 cells with CDs, CDs@ Fe3+ and CDs@Fe3+@ F−. Scale bar: 100 μm.
Fluorescence confocal images of E. coli cells treated with CDs, CDs@Fe3+ and CDs@Fe3+@ F−, respectively.
Logic scheme of the ‘‘AND’’ logic gate (A) and the truth table (B).
Schematic of the preparation of CDs and their application for Fe3+ and F−.
Comparison of different fluorescent CDs based probes for Fe3+ detection.
Recoveries of Fe3+ and F− in human blood serum samples detected by the proposed method (n = 6).

head.ce:title: “On-off-on” detection of Fe3+ and F−, biological imaging, and its logic gate operation based on excitation-independent blue-fluorescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Center of Environmental Science and Engineering Research, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Center of Environmental Science and Engineering Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, and Center of Environmental Science and Engineering Research, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science and Engineering, James Cook University, Townsville, Queensland, 4811, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science and Engineering, James Cook University, Townsville, Queensland 4811, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent nanoprobe based on carbon dots (CDs) has been facilely synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal pyrolysis of salicylic acid and utilized for the sequential detection of Fe3+ and F− in vitro. The fluorescence of CDs can be extinguished dramatically by Fe3+ based on static quenching and subsequently recovery upon addition of F− due to the formation of stabler FeF3. The probe exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity toward Fe3+ and F− with a good linearity in the range of 10–300 μM and 0.1–200 μM, respectively, and a low detection limit of 52 nM and 8.5 nM, respectively. More importantly, as-prepared CDs with exceedingly fluorescence stability, negligible toxicity and superior biocompatibility have been expanded for detection Fe3+ and F− in living cell and Escherichia coli. Furthermore, an “AND” logic gate based on as-obtained CDs has been constructed.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron, the most abundant metal element in the earth’scrust, exhibits the vital importance for many biological processes, including oxygen transportation, transcriptional regulation, DNA replication and electron transfer [1–5]. Inadequate or excessive iron intake enable to cause some diseases and even be life threatening. Lack of Fe3+ is linked with diseases of aging like Alzheimer’s and other neurogenerative diseases [6,7]. Whereas excess of Fe3+ intake leads to tissue damage due to the formation of free radicals [8–11]. Thus, the determination of Fe3+ is of fundamental importance in clinic.

Fluoride (F−) is an essential element of the human body and plays a key role in human health and medical processes [12]. Fluoride is a common additive in the toothpaste, pharmaceutical agents and even drinking water, bacause a specific amount of fluoride is benefitial to dental care and clinical treatment for osteoporosis [13]. Nevertheless, abnormal content of F− not only do harm to aquatic organisms and plants, but also induce numerous health problems to human beings, such as dental and skeletal fluorosis, acute gastric and kidney disorders, and DNA damage [14–17]. In recent years, various methods for the detection of F− have been reported, including ion chromatography [18], gas chromatographic [19], colorimetric [20] and 19F NMR [21]. However, these methods usually exhibit some unavoidable drawbacks such as tedious sample preparation procedures, long determination time, low sensitivity, and high cost, which seriously limit their practical applications. Therefore, it is highly imperative to develop accurate, reliable methods for the determination of F−.

Carbon dots (CDs), as the emerging nanomaterials, have attracted much attention owing to their distinctive merits such as easy preparation and functionalization, good water solubility, high biocompatibility, and low toxicity [22–27]. CDs-based fluorescence nanoprobes have been used for the determination ofmultiple targets including pH [28], Al3+ [29], Cu2+ [30], Fe3+ [31], Ag+ [32], NO3− [33], S2− [34], cysteine [35], glucose [36], antibiotics [37], etc. Numerous CDs systems are constructed for a single analyte. Recently, much effort is made for simultaneous detecting multiple targets. Wu et al. [38] developed an “turn–on” approach for highly selective recognition of Hg2+ and Ag+ using CDs as a bifunctional sensing substrate based on ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) induced fluorescent recovery. Du et al. [39] introduced ascorbic acid (AA) as a reductant toward Fe3+ to design a muli-functional CDs fluorescence probe for specificity determination Fe3+ and AA. Gao et al. [40] designed CDs as a dual mode sensor for sequential detection of Cr(VI) and cysteine and demonstrated their utilization in bioimaging. From then on, other researchers [41–44] also constructed CDs-based dual sensor fluorescent probes, which broadened the fileds for investigating various targets. However, most of these fluorescent CDs-based probes are typically fabricated with more than one staring material, which requires complicated separation and purification. One of the convenient strategies for the preparation of versatile CDs, reported recently, is only using 1,2,4-triaminobenzene as carbon precursor by a facile solvothermal method, which successfully utilized to detect of Ag+ and cysteine by an “on-off-on” fluorescence response [45]. Unfortunately, the fluorescence quantum yields (QY) is not impressive (≤15%), nailing down their applicability in living cells. Therefore, the development of high-efficient bifunctional CDs with facile preparation and good luminescence behaviour are still of important concern.

In our work, a multi-functional sensing platform based on CDs for detection of Fe3+ and F− has been designed. The CDs were manufactured by a simple hydrothermal approach using salicylic acid as a carbon source. The obtained CDs possess excellent water-solubility, fluorescence stability, and good photoluminescence (PL) properties with the quantum yield of 20.6%. Moreover, the CDs were found to reveal an attracting “on-off-on” three-state emission with the stepwise addition of Fe3+ and F−. The detection of Fe3+ is based on the formation of a CDs@ Fe3+ complex, while F− can effectively recover the fluorescence owing to their ability to remove Fe3+ from the surface of CDs. Based on the above results, the proposed strategy has been successfully employed in vitro sequential detection of Fe3+ and F−. Addtionally, an “AND” logic gate has been established (see Scheme 1 ).

CDs were synthesized with salicylic acid as carbon source by a one−pot hydrothermal carbonization method. Briefly, 0.2 g of salicylic acid was dissolved in 10 mL of DDI water at room temperature. Then the solution was transferred to the Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and maintained at 200 °C for 4 h. After the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally, the solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 min to remove large particles and the supernatant was collected. Subsequently, CDs aqueous solution was subjected to dialysis for 24 h. The purified CDs were kept at 4 °C for further use.

The concentration of CDs was diluted to 0.05 mg mL−1 with pH 7.4 phosphate buffered saline (PBS), then different amounts of Fe3+ were added, respectively and mixed thoroughly. After incubating at room temperature for 1 min, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded. For the detection of F−, the CDs solution (0.05 mg mL−1) was first mixed with Fe3+ (300 μM), and then added different concentrations of F−. To investigate the selectivity of the probe for Fe3+ and F−, a variety of metal ions or anions were separately introduced into the CDs solution and the fluorescence spectra were recorded. All the samples were excited (λex) at 320 nm, and the intensities of fluorescence emission (λem) at 425 nm for each sample were recorded for comparison.

Fresh human blood samples were obtained from local hospital. In brief, 1 mL blood was initially mixed with 5 mL of Red Blood Cell (RBC) lysis buffer, and incubated at room temperature for 15 min. The lysed samples were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to separate the proteins from the serum, and then the supernatant was collected. Then, 0.2 mL 20% ascorbic acid was added to the above supernatant to oxidize Fe2+ to Fe3+ for further analysis. Finally, the prepared samples were used for detections of Fe3+ and F− according to the general procedure without additional special treatment.

The Human hepatoma cellular carcinoma (SMMC-7721) cells were cultured in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle medium (DMEM) and the cells were plated on culture dish in an atmosphere of 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, the SMMC-7721 cells were incubated with 0.05 mg mL−1 CDs for 3 h. Then the SMMC-7721 cells were rinsed with pH 7.4 PBS for three times and kept in PBS for optical imaging by Zeiss LSM880 + Airgscan confocal laser-scanning microscope. Next, the CDs-stained SMMC-7721 cells were treated by sequentially adding 10 μL Fe3+ (10.0 mM) and 10 μL F− (10.0 mM).

The morphology and size of the as-synthesized CDs were characterized by TEM (Fig. 1 ). As shown in Fig. 1A, B and C, CDs are monodisperse spheres and well distributed. The histogram in Fig. 1D clearly displays that the size distribution falls within the range from 3 to 8 nm and a mean particle diameter is between 4.92 ± 0.31 nm, while CDs showed no obvious crystal lattice, suggesting an amorphous structure.

Fig. 2A shows the Raman spectrum of synthesized CDs. D band (1396 cm−1) and G band (1533 cm−1), two signature peaks for carbon, can be clearly detected for CDs, corresponding to disordered structure in crystalline of the plane graphite sp3 and the sp2 cluster, respectively. As been proved, the intensity ratio of ID/IG indicates the ratio of sp3/sp2 carbon. The calculated ID/IG ratio for the prepared CDs was 0.91, implying that they have a structure similar to graphite.

Then, the elemental analysis and surface composition of the CDs was explored. As shown in Fig. 2 B, two dominant peaks at 281.5 and 529.1 eV in the XPS survey spectrum matched with C1s and O1s, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum in Fig. 2C predominantly exhibited three peaks at 284.8, 286.3 and 288.6 eV, which ascribed to C1s states in C–C/C=C, C–O, C=O, respectively. This indicates that the CDs are rich in hydrophilic groups on the surfaces. The O1s spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2D) can be analyzed as two peaks at 531.2 and 532.4 eV, correspond to the presence of C=O, C–O–C/C–OH in the CDs.

FTIR measurements were conducted to characterize the chemical structure of CDs and the result was shown in the Fig. 2E. CDs shows that peaks at 2918, 2848 and 1463 cm−1 correspond to stretching vibration of C–H. The peak at 1582 cm−1 is ascribed to C=O, and the peaks at 1365 cm−1 and 1222 cm−1 indicate the existence of O–H. The surface components of the CDs determined by the FT-IR further confirmed that results of XPS.

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were utilized to explore the fluorescence properties of CDs. The UV–vis absorption spectrum(black line in Fig. 3 A) of CDs in aqueous solution possesses a strong absorption peak at 268 nm together with a shoulder around 207 nm corresponding to the n−π* transition of the aromatic π system containing the C=O band and π−π* transition of the aromatic C=C bands by sp2 domains. The red and blue lines in Fig. 3A were the PL spectra of CDs, which can be seen that the excitation (λex) and emission (λem) peaks are located at 320 and 425 nm, respectively.

In quest of further exploring the optical properties of the as-synthesized CDs, detailed PL spectra of CDs were also acquired under different excitation wavelengths in Fig. 3B. The emission peak of CDs solutions shows no shift and remains at 425 nm when the excitation wavelength changes from 300 to 350 nm. Furthermore, as shown in Table S1, the fluorescence quantum yield (Φs) of the as-prepared CDs was calculated to be 20.6% under the optimized conditions (200 °C, 4 h), by choosing Quinine sulfate in ethanol as the reference. The high quantum yield of as-prepared CDs should be attributed to the presence of hydroxyl group which may contribute to form H-bonds, provide enhanced rigidity of the nanoplate structure, and thus assists to suppress non-radiative decay channels [46].

To evaluate the optical stability of CDs, we investigated the effects of multiple variables. As depicted in Fig. S1A, FL intensity remains unvarying under different concentrations of KCl, indicating that CDs could be used under physical salt conditions in practical applications. The high resistance to photo-bleaching of the CDs is shown in Fig. S1B. The fluorescence intensity showed almost no noticeable change even after 50 min exposure to xenon arc light. It can be observed that, three months of storaged in the surrounding conditions, the color of the CDs solution remain unchanged without obvious precipitation and they still show the luminescence property (Fig. S1C). These results exhibit the wonderful photostability of the fluorescent probe and its long-term colloidal stability which exists great potential for carriage. Fig. S2 displays the effect of pH on the PL intensity of CDs. The as-synthesized CDs are stable from pH 2.0–10.0, and this pH range is suitable for bio-imaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3+detection of CDs in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quenching process refers to the interaction between the fluorescent molecules and the quencher molecules, which usually originates from dynamic or static quenching. Fluorescence lifetimes can discriminate between dynamic and static quenching. Thus, fluorescence decay curves of CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+ were tested (Fig. S3). The lifetimes of CDs and CDs/Fe3+ are calculated to be 15.32 ns and 15.20 ns, respectively. This nearly identical values confirmed the PL quenching of CDs was caused by static quenching. In addition, two other evidences (absorption and zeta-potential) also elucidated the mechanism. UV–vis absorption experiments of CDs with various concentrations of Fe3+ were performed (Fig. S4). Particularly, the addition of Fe3+ caused a new peak around 300 nm which showed a significant absorption enhancement as the Fe3+ concentration increased. We speculate that fluorescence quenching of CDs by Fe3+ could be assigned to complexes formed between Fe3+ and carboxyl/hydroxyl groups on the CDs. As depicted in Fig. S5, the zeta potential value changed from −30.4 to +17.7 mV after addition of Fe3+, which demonstrated that Fe3+ was selectively coordinated with the negative groups on as-obtained CDs. These results suggest that static quenching occurred between CDs and Fe3+. In this process, Fe3+ interact with the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CDs to form nonfluorescent complexes which result in the change of surface defect emission of CDs [52,53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: F− detection of CDs in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, the quenched fluorescence by metal ions binding may be reversibly restored by adding more competitive counterpart ions. Surprisingly, we found that the quenched fluorescence of the CDs–Fe3+ can be basically recovered with the addition of F−. But there was no significant effect on the PL intensity of CDs in the presence of.

F− (Fig. S6). Therefore, the as-prepared CDs were discovered to display a diverting “on-off-on” three-state emission with the stepwise addition of Fe3+ and F−. As display in Fig. S8, that the fluorescence color of CDs solution changes from blue to colorless with the increase of Fe3+ concentration while the fluorescence is gradually recovered with increasing concentrations of F−. In this work, to investigate the feasibility of the CDs–Fe3+ system and the fluorescence enhancement, different kinds of tolerance ions (including Cl−, Br−, I−, NO3 −, CN−, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, PO4 3−, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, SCN−, S2−, HS−, AA and EDTA) were investigated as control samples. However, none of these control substances had obvious influences on the fluorometric responses, only F− can make the fluorescence intensity regain (Fig. 5 A). This phenomenon probably could be attributed to the fact that stable ionic compounds (FeF3) were formed when F− was added to a solution of the CDs–Fe3+ complex, which were stable in aqueous solution due to the high lattice energy [54]. As can be seen for CDs@Fe3+@F− in Fig. S7, the band at 300 nm disappeared, which is probably attributable to the highefficiency chelation result of the functional groups in F−, so that the Fe3+ on the surface of the CDs were detached. As a result, Fe3+ could be removed from the surface of CDs, thereby recovering the fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence spectra of the CDs–Fe3+ (300 μmol L−1) system in the presence of different concentrations of F− were recorded after incubation for 10 min. As shown in Fig. 5B, the fluorescence of the system enhanced gradually with increasing the F− concentration from 0.1 to 400 μmol L−1. Furthermore, A good linearity is observed between ΔF and F− over the concentration range of 0.1–200 μM with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.993 (insert in Fig. 5B). The detection limit is estimated to be 8.5 nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ and F− in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: For testing the practicality of this developed approach, standard recovery experiments were performed in human blood serum samples (Table 2 ). As presented in Table 2, the recoveries of all samples were in the ranges 94.60%–100.55%, and the RSDs were in the ranges 0.89%–1.18%. These results strongly validated that the proposed method may broaden ways for practical detections of Fe3+ and F− in real samples.

The excitation-independent blue-fluorescent CDs suggests the potential for bio-imaging. We selected SMMC-7721 cells as the model by the standard cell viability MTT assay to evaluate the cytotoxic effect of the CDs. In the MTT assay, the quantitative information about the cytotoxicity of CDs can be acquired by measuring the absorption on the SMMC-7721 cells. As shown in Fig. S9, more than 82% cells could survive after incubated with concentrations of CDs from 0.1 to 1.0 mg mL−1. The results indicated that the prepared CDs have low toxicity to culture cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3+ detection of CDs in living cells and E. coli cells
ce:section.ce:para: We have also explored the use of CDs as a fluorescent imaging probe for Escherichia coli (E. coli). Fig. 8 shows the images of the CDs labelled E. coli. In Fig. 8, these images revealed that CDs can efficiently attach to the bacterial cells. After adding 0.3 mM Fe3+, an apparent fluorescence intensity disappearance was discovered. A fluorescence signal could be renewed with 0.4 mM F− in the E. coli. These results show that the CDs can be potentially used for in vitro E. coli cell labelling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Logic operation of CDs towards Fe3+ and F−
ce:section.ce:para: On account of the significant fluorescence restoration of the CDs-Fe3+ system with the addition of F−, we devised an “AND” molecular logic gate. Fe3+ and F− were acted as two inputs and restored FL intensity at 425 nm taken as the optical output in this logic gate. Fig. 9 displayed the homologous symbol and truth table of the results of each input mode. For the output, the recovery of fluorescence intensity and no fluorescence recovery were defined as “1” and “0”, respectively. For the input, the presence and absence of Fe3+and F− are assigned as “1” and “0”, respectively. Thus, the gate (output) was activated and emited an output signal of “1” only in the existence of both Fe3+and F− (1, 1).

Excitation-independent blue-fluorescent CDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using salicylic acid as carbon precursor without further surface passivation or modification. As-synthesized CDs display excellent stability under various conditions, including pH, ionic strength, and continuous excitation. Interestingly, obtained CDs have been developed as bifunctional fluorescent probe for rapid, precise, and quantitative detection of Fe3+ and F−. Fe3+ can effectively quench the fluorescence emission of CDs due to its complexation with functional groups of the CDs surface. Subsequently, the addition of F− results in fluorescence recovery of CDs because a stabler ionic compound (FeF3) was formed. Bestowed with excellent biocompatibility and low cell cytotoxicity, the fluorescent nanoprobe has been applied to imaging Fe3+ and F− in biosystems. Additionally, an “AND” logic gate at the molecular level has been constructed. We prospect that the proposed strategy will offer a promising platform in biomedical, environmental, and photoelectric fields.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874087 and 21575084), and the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (201701D121019). We also acknowledge Dr. Juanjuan Wang for her help with Zeiss LSM880 confocal laser-scanning microscope measurements from the Scientific Instrument Center at Shanxi University.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.117716.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly stable and selective measurement of Fe3+ ions under environmentally relevant conditions via an excitation-based multiwavelength method using N, S-doped carbon dots

J. Lumin.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and evaluation of a new furfuran-based rhodamine B fluorescent chemosensor for selective detection of Fe3+ and its application in living-cell imaging
Sens. Actuators, B
